policy_statement: TMG will support disaster waste planning and wide-area cooperation for proper disposal and promote systems for temporary housing and emergency repairs after disasters.
mechanism_description: TMG will support disaster waste disposal planning and wide-area cooperation and promote systems for temporary housing and emergency repairs to speed recovery and improve resilience after disasters.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard/disaster → improved institutional preparedness + faster recovery → ↓ exposure/vulnerability + ↓ disruption impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Governments and partner jurisdictions implement disaster waste management plans, wide-area cooperation agreements, and pre-arranged systems for temporary housing and rapid emergency repairs.  
(2) Mediating process: Faster debris clearance, reduced secondary public health/environmental harms, quicker restoration of shelter and critical services, and improved coordination/logistics across jurisdictions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Increased disaster resilience—shorter recovery times and lower societal/economic damages from extreme events that are more frequent/severe under climate change.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced impacts (exposure, disruption, and recovery time) from climate-amplified disasters through better preparedness and coordinated recovery systems.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism targets disaster preparedness and post-disaster recovery capacity, which primarily reduces vulnerability and speeds recovery from climate hazards.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While improved disaster waste handling can incidentally affect emissions (e.g., reduced open burning, better landfill management), the dominant intent and effect across such policies is minimizing disaster impacts and accelerating recovery—an Adaptation outcome.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific implementation mandates low-carbon debris processing or methane capture, that would add Mitigation co-benefits, but those are not the core/common pathway implied by “planning, cooperation, temporary housing, emergency repairs.”  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism—planning and coordination for disaster waste disposal plus temporary housing/repairs—primarily functions to improve resilience to disasters, so Adaptation remains the correct category across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Any secondary classification (e.g., mitigation co-benefits via waste treatment choices) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on explicit evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could have mitigation co-benefits depending on disaster debris processing (e.g., recycling vs. landfilling, methane controls) and reconstruction standards, but the canonical mechanism as stated is institutional resilience and recovery speed (Adaptation).
key_indicators: "support the formulation of disaster waste disposal plans and manuals"; "carry out wide-area collaboration and cooperation"; "promote the development of a system to provide temporary housing and emergency repairs in the event of a disaster"; "early restoration and recovery"; "dispose of disaster waste properly, smoothly, and promptly"
co_benefits: Public health protection (reduced contamination/vector risks from debris); environmental protection (proper disposal); economic continuity (faster reopening/recovery)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted partly as infrastructure-investment if the “system” implies physical stockpiles/facilities for temporary housing, but the text emphasizes planning/support and coordination rather than specific capital works.
